The PKGBUILD contains only variable assignments and array definitions at the top level. No command substitutions, function calls, or other executable code exist outside of the `package()` function, which is not executed by `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The `pkgver()` function is not defined, so no code runs there. All source URLs point to the project's own GitHub repository over HTTPS, and checksums are provided (not SKIP), ensuring integrity. There is no malicious code that would execute during the source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing steps.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code executes during source fetch or sourcing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/scalpel-poe-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-hw5_xwyh...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-hw5_xwyh
Analyzing scalpel-poe-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-hw5_xwyh) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources fetched over HTTPS from the project's own GitHub repository: an AppImage binary, an icon, a license file, and two local scripts (scalpel-poe.sh and scalpel-poe.desktop). All URLs are constructed using the declared upstream version and the official `url` variable, pointing to release assets and raw file paths in the same repository. The `sha256sums` are provided and match the sources, ensuring integrity. There is no command substitution, dynamic fetching via curl/wget, or execution of remote code in the global scope or in `pkgver()`. The `pkgver` is hardcoded, so no external network access occurs during version resolution. Sourcing the PKGBUILD for `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `--nobuild` will only evaluate static variables and download verifiable, pinned sources via HTTPS. No data exfiltration, obfuscated logic, or runtime execution occurs during this phase.
</details>
<summary>
Safe sourcing and source fetching with HTTPS and checksums.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe sourcing and source fetching with HTTPS and checksums.[/]

makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful

makepkg --printsrcinfo successful

[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, scalpel-poe.desktop...
LLM auditresponse for scalpel-poe.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `scalpel-poe.desktop` is a standard desktop entry file used to integrate the application into the desktop environment's menu system. It specifies metadata such as the application name, comment, executable command, icon, and categories. The `Exec` field invokes `scalpel-poe %U`, which is a normal pattern for launching desktop applications with optional URL arguments. The `Icon` and `StartupWMClass` fields are correctly set to application-specific values, aiding in proper window manager grouping and icon resolution.

There are no suspicious commands, embedded scripts, or network operations. The use of `Terminal=false` indicates it is a GUI application, which aligns with typical desktop entry behavior. The file does not perform any file modifications, execute shell commands, or include obfuscated content. It poses no security risk and conforms entirely to freedesktop.org desktop entry standards.

All fields are appropriate for a game-related application and consistent with the package's purpose. No data exfiltration, unauthorized execution, or supply-chain attack vectors are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry, no security issues
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed scalpel-poe.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard desktop entry, no security issues
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard Arch Linux package build process for distributing a pre-built AppImage of the "Scalpel" tool for Path of Exile. The sources are fetched from the official GitHub repository (https://github.com/scalpelpoe/scalpel) over HTTPS, including the AppImage binary, icon, license, desktop file, and a helper script. All downloads use verifiable URLs pointing to specific tagged releases, ensuring reproducibility.

Checksums (sha256sums) are provided for all source files, including the AppImage, which prevents silent corruption or tampering. The package installs files to standard system locations: the AppImage to /opt, the launcher script to /usr/bin, icons to /usr/share/pixmaps, and licenses to /usr/share/licenses. No network-fetching commands (like curl|bash) or code downloads occur during build or install stages. The `options=(!strip)` directive is appropriate for binaries where symbol stripping could break functionality.

There is no use of eval, base64 decoding, or obfuscated payloads. The package does not modify system files outside its scope, nor does it execute unauthorized code. The dependencies and file placements align with typical packaging practices for AppImage-based software. While the maintainer email is listed in an unconventional format, this does not constitute a security issue.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AppImage packaging from verified GitHub release
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

LLM auditresponse for LICENSE:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file content is the full text of the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0), a well-known free software license. It contains no executable code, scripts, or commands—only legal text describing the terms under which software may be used, modified, and distributed. 

This license is commonly included in open-source software packages, including those distributed via the AUR, to clarify the licensing status of the software. The presence of this file alone poses no security risk. There are no network requests, no file operations, no obfuscated content, and no potentially dangerous constructs.

The decision is SAFE because this is a standard, legitimate license file that does not exhibit any malicious behavior or supply-chain attack characteristics.
</details>
<summary>
Standard open-source license file, no executable code.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing scalpel-poe.sh...
+ Reviewed LICENSE. Status: SAFE -- Standard open-source license file, no executable code.
LLM auditresponse for scalpel-poe.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `scalpel-poe.sh` sets the `XDG_SESSION_TYPE` environment variable to `x11` and then executes the `Scalpel.AppImage` binary located in `/opt/scalpel-poe/` with the `--ozone-platform=x11` flag and any additional arguments passed to the script. The use of `exec` ensures that the process replaces the script itself, which is a standard practice for launchers.

There are no network operations, file manipulations, or system modifications present in the script. It does not employ obfuscation, dangerous commands like `eval`, or execute downloaded content. The behavior is consistent with a typical launcher script for an AppImage-based application, ensuring compatibility with X11 by enforcing the platform flag and session type.

The script operates within the expected scope of application launch and environment configuration. No data exfiltration, unauthorized access, or persistent changes are performed. As such, it does not exhibit malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Launch script for AppImage; no malicious activity found</summary>
</security_assessment>
